coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of efficient stable dendrimer-modified carbon for cleaner drilling shale inhibition
coredata.dc:creator: Ibrahim, Mukaila A.
Saleh, Tawfik A.
coredata.dc:description: Shale hydration is a major hurdle in oil well drilling with water-based drilling fluids (WBDFs) in oil and gas which could greatly increase the gross cost of production due to well collapse. Shale hydration inhibitors must be added to the WBDFs to prevent such occurrence. Attention has been drawn currently to the design of eco-friendly and low-cost shale inhibitors having excellent compatibility with WBDFs for effective wellbore drilling operations. Herein we report the efficient enhancement of shale inhibition of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) by incorporating activated carbon modified dendrimer (ACD) represented as ACD/PVP with a low environmental footprint. The characterization of the composite material, as well as the sodium bentonite (Na-BT) treated with it via various spectroscopic and imaging instruments including FTIR, NMR, TGA, and SEM, was established. The result of the TGA revealed that the materials could withstand high temperatures. SEM images proved that it has the tendency to successfully inhibiting shale swelling. The prepared material shale preventive ability was further assessed through an anti-swelling ratio, recovery test, and free swelling test. The 2 wt% AC-D/PVP solution reflected outstanding shale inhibition and tolerance to high temperature. This could be ascribed to the synergetic activity of the composite in which the dendrimer acted as a cation, which brings the clay silica sheets together while the PVP participated in sealing the surface of the clay mineral via the large volume of hydrogen bond formation with the dendrimer to form a shield against water penetration. The plugging potential of AC and the hydrophobic character of the PVP polymer chain also contributed greatly to this effect. The PVP which is the major component of the materials is environmentally compatible likewise the AC-D to some extent. In view of this, the AC-D/PVP composite could be a suitable substitute for the present less efficient and unsustainable shale inhibitors that are being used in the oil and gas industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cleaner drilling
Sustainability
Composite engineering
Carbon dots
Dendrimer
Polyvinylpyrrolidone
Shale
Drilling fluid engineering

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Shale inhibition ability by immersion examination

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of the activated carbon modified dendrimer (AC-D).
Synthesis of the activated carbon modified dendrimer /polyvinylpyrrolidone composite.
The FTIR spectra of AC-D against AD-C/PVP composite.
Anti-swelling ratio (a) and shale recovery test (b) results in 2 wt% AC-D/PVP, and 10 wt% KCl.
Image of (a) Na-Bt pellet, the pellet when immersed in (b) 10 wt% KCl (c) 2 wt% AC-D/PVP and after 24 h of immersion in (d) water (e) 10 wt% KCl (f) 2 wt% AC-D/PVP.
SEM image and EDX spectra of (a, b) plain Na-Bt, (c, d) Na-Bt after contact with 2.0 wt% AC-D/PVP after 24 h.
A suggested mechanism of shale inhibition by AC-D/PVP.

row.entry: to reduce fluid loss of drilling fluid
Antifoam, foam breaker, hinder the entrainment of a gas in a liquid

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: AV – Average viscosity; PV – Plastic viscosity; YP – Yield point; Gel10 s – Gel strength at 10 s;
Gel10 m – Gel strength at 10 min.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of efficient stable dendrimer-modified carbon for cleaner drilling shale inhibition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, King Fahd University of Petroleum & Minerals, Dhahran, 31261, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: King Fahd University of Petroleum & Minerals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 1Department of Chemistry, King Fahd University of Petroleum & Minerals, Dhahran 31261, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, University of Lethbridge, 4401University Drive, Lethbridge, Alberta, T1K 3M4, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, University of Lethbridge, 4401University Drive, Lethbridge, Alberta, Canada, T1K 3M4

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Shale hydration is a major hurdle in oil well drilling with water-based drilling fluids (WBDFs) in oil and gas which could greatly increase the gross cost of production due to well collapse. Shale hydration inhibitors must be added to the WBDFs to prevent such occurrence. Attention has been drawn currently to the design of eco-friendly and low-cost shale inhibitors having excellent compatibility with WBDFs for effective wellbore drilling operations. Herein we report the efficient enhancement of shale inhibition of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) by incorporating activated carbon modified dendrimer (ACD) represented as ACD/PVP with a low environmental footprint. The characterization of the composite material, as well as the sodium bentonite (Na-BT) treated with it via various spectroscopic and imaging instruments including FTIR, NMR, TGA, and SEM, was established. The result of the TGA revealed that the materials could withstand high temperatures. SEM images proved that it has the tendency to successfully inhibiting shale swelling. The prepared material shale preventive ability was further assessed through an anti-swelling ratio, recovery test, and free swelling test. The 2 wt% AC-D/PVP solution reflected outstanding shale inhibition and tolerance to high temperature. This could be ascribed to the synergetic activity of the composite in which the dendrimer acted as a cation, which brings the clay silica sheets together while the PVP participated in sealing the surface of the clay mineral via the large volume of hydrogen bond formation with the dendrimer to form a shield against water penetration. The plugging potential of AC and the hydrophobic character of the PVP polymer chain also contributed greatly to this effect. The PVP which is the major component of the materials is environmentally compatible likewise the AC-D to some extent. In view of this, the AC-D/PVP composite could be a suitable substitute for the present less efficient and unsustainable shale inhibitors that are being used in the oil and gas industry.

ce:section.ce:para: The rising demand for oil and gas led to the search for other sources including shale gas exploration and exploitation [1,2]. To reach and tap the oil and gas deposit in any geological housing the reserve, drilling of a suitable well is required. Drilling fluids or muds of different solvent (oil or water) based is necessary for the well drilling to lubricate and clean the drilling bit, transport drilling cuttings from downhole to the surface, maintain pressure or prevent pressure loss in the borehole, prevent fluid loss into shale formation and ultimately address the problem of shale hydration [3,4]. Shale hydration is a phenomenon in which the free water in water-based muds (WBMs) interact with the clay minerals in shale, which consequently results in swelling, weakening, and disintegration of the shale formation zone of oil wells.

Though oil-based muds (OBMs) and synthetic-based muds (SBMs) have good performance such as excellent shale inhibition, lubricity, temperature tolerance, and rheological properties compared to the water-based muds (WBMs) [5–8], however, they have a potential threat to the environment in addition to their high cost which has limited their application in the drilling processes except in the case where their use is inevitable particularly in deep-sea extremely sensitive shale drilling [9,10].

The WBMs have their advantages and disadvantages [11]. They are somehow economical and eco-friendly, but a sequence of problems is usually faced by formation being drilled with them as a result of shale clay mineral interaction with free water in the fluid. Such problems include high drag with torque, bit balling, cuttings dispersion, caving, sloughing, stuck pile, wellbore collapse, reduced drilling rate, and circulation loss [3,12,13]. The overall consequence is the excessive costs of well drilling operation due to well caving and collapse [14]. Therefore, shale inhibitors are usually incorporated in the WBMs for perfect well drilling and cleaning to minimize hydration or swelling of the shale minerals. The conventional KCl being used globally as shale inhibitor has been banned due to the high concentration involved which subsequently raises its environmental impact [15].

Myriad authors have explored a series of inorganic and organic molecules as possible replacements for KCl to inhibit shale swelling in the last three decades [16]. These include inorganic salts [17], amines/amides/imines compounds and their polymers [18–20], ionic liquids [21,22], natural and modified natural polymers such as gelatin [23], starch [24], chitosan [25], plant extracts [26,27], as well as synthetic polymers and copolymers including partially hydrolyzed polyacrylamide (PHPA) [28], sulfonated copolymers [29,30] etc. These materials face challenges such as low efficiency to inhibit shale hydration, instability at high temperature, incompatibility with additives, high cost, and environmental limitation [31,32]. Recently, nanomaterials such as silica [33] and graphene [34] have been proven to be capable of plugging nanopores and nanofractures in the shale formation to prevent borehole instability with remarkable stability at high temperature [35]. Their effectiveness is even enhanced when a composite of the nanomaterials is used.

Activated carbon (AC) has large surface area and porosity. The functionalization of the AC surface has also been proven to enhance its application [36,37]. Aside from being inexpensive, it is highly abundant and could be generated from a wide range of sources including wastes such as tyers and polymers [38–40]. Method for converting scrap tires and other polymeric wastes to highly pure activated carbon had been developed [41]. Its application ranges from adsorption to catalysis [42–45].

Amine based polymer and oligomers such as dendrimers, hydrocarbon diamines, lipophilic amine polymers, amino acids, polyamines, and polyethoxylated amines activeness as shale inhibitors are generally affected by pH variation [46]. They tend to undergo deprotonation as the pH increases which is a major property of amine-terminated compounds. Since the pH of conventional mud formulation in oil and gas drilling is about 8.4, the activities of amine-based clay minerals inhibitors are inevitably hindered [20]. Therefore, the use of an amine-based compound that is less susceptible to protonation-deprotonation will be a quite spectacular step in designing a suitable shale hydration inhibitor. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) is an amphiphilic biocompatible water-soluble polymer that is widely used in various industrial applications such as cosmetics and even in drug delivery and as food additives [47]. The cyclic hydrophilic amide ring and the hydrophobic methylene/methine polymer backbone provides it with the amphiphilic property [48,49]. The integration of dendrimer in PVP is very useful in drug delivery which establishes its application as an environmentally sustainable material in industries [49].

When modified with dendrimer (D), AC activity gets improved having a high hydrodynamic volume in solution which boosts its interaction with various functional groups through hydrogen bond formation [50–52]. In this work for the first time, we prepared a dendrimer functionalized activated carbon (AC-D), and AC-D modified with polyvinylpyrrolidone (AC-D/PVP) composite to explore their shale inhibitive power. The inhibition tests and imaging characterization illustrates that the matrix performed exceptionally in retarding water interaction with shale minerals. This could be linked to the high hydrogen bond density that could be formed between the dendrimer, the PVP, and the clay surface to form a preventive film towards the water in mud; the plugging potential of AC, and the hydrophobic character of the PVP polymer chain.

The AC was prepared from waste tires via a method in the literature [43]. Ethylenediamine (EDA, ≥98 %), ethanol (≥99 %), and methyl methacrylate (≥99 %) were purchased from Fluka Chemical, Switzerland. Vinylpyrrolidone (≥99 %), Azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN, ≥99 %), and methanol (≥99 %) were supply by Sigma Aldrich, USA. Nitric acid was provided by BDH Chemical, UK. All chemicals were used as received.

The preparation of the AC-D was accomplished with a standard method with slight modification [53]. The first step involved the introduction of carboxylic groups on the surface of AC to form AC-COOH [54]. The second step resulted in the conversion of the carboxylic surface to dendrimer generation (Fig. 1 ). Typically, 500 mg of AC-COOH was put in a round bottom flask containing 150 ml methanol and sonicated for 15 min for adequate dispersion of the material. The flask was then transferred to an oil bath, equipped with a nitrogen flow, and subjected to stirring. About 5 g EDA was added to the AC-COOH suspension gradually over a period of 30 min and stirred for 24 h. Methyl methacrylate of 37 g was gradually added to the mixture, stirred at 0 °C for 30 min and allow to go back to room temperature upon which it was further stirred for 24 h. The final product was retrieved, washed with ethanol, and dried at 40 °C for 12 h.

The AC-D/PVP composite was prepared by free radical polymerization [55]. The AC-D (250 mg) was dispersed in 100 ml ethanol and subjected to stirring in a three-necked flask in an oil bath. This was followed by the addition of 10 g of PVP and purged with N2 gas for 15 min in order to eliminate dissolved O2. The reaction was initiated by adding 1.5 g AIBN dissolved in 5 ml ethanol, and the reaction was allowed to run at 60 °C for 15 h. The composite product obtained was precipitated in cold ethyl acetate under stirring. The precipitate was filtered and dried for 24 h at 60 °C (Fig. 2 ).

Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10 Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) spectrophotometer was utilized to determine IR spectra of the dendrimer functionalized activated carbon (AC-D), and AC-D modified with polyvinylpyrrolidone (AC-D/PVP). Potassium bromide pellets of 2.50 cm diameter containing about 5 wt% material were prepared. The spectra were then obtained by scanning the samples; wavenumber 4000 − 500 cm−1, 4 cm−1 resolution, 16 scans. Moreover, FTIR analysis was supported with proton NMR (1HNMR) to reflect the successful preparation of the products. The spectral was recorded using 400 MHz Brucker AV III 400 spectrometer in D2O.

The morphology of the bentonite pellet’s surface before and after interacting with the inhibitor was evaluated with the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) FESEM/FIB/GIS (Tescan Lyra-3) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX or EDS) analyzer. The thermal stability of the prepared material was evaluated by thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA) SDT-Q600 Simultaneous, under N2 atmosphere.

The anti-swelling ratio (AR) measurement was carried out in accordance with China’s Natural Gas Industry Standard SY/T 5971-94 for measuring clay stabilization in drilling fluids. Around 1 g of Na-Bt has dispersed 20.0 ml of composites solution and then left for 120 min. The suspension was centrifuged at approximately 1500 rpm until the fluid phase is clearly separated from the clay particles. The change in the volume of bentonite was recorded and AR was calculated as:
(1)
A R =
V W
-
V P
V W
-
V O
where VP and VW are bentonite change in the volume in prepared material solution and in the water. VO is the bentonite volume in kerosene [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Shale inhibition ability by immersion examination
ce:section.ce:para: The immersion study was carried out on Na-Bt in the presence of the developed material. The results were compared with swelling of the Na-Bt in both water and potassium chloride (KCl) to establish the efficacy of the composite in efficient inhibition of shale. In this study, Na-Bt powder was first dried at 105 °C for 4 h to eliminate adsorbed moisture. The Na-Bt powders were then weighed into 10 g portions that were compressed into pellet discs of 5.0 cm under 10 MPa using a hydraulic presser for 5 min. for the test. The pellets were submerged into 20 ml each of deionized water, 10 wt% KCl solution, 2 wt % AC-D and 2 wt % AC-D/PVP. The 2 wt % concentration of the inhibitor was selected upon optimization. The immersed samples volume was measured after 16 h.

A shale recovery test was conducted in the solution of each inhibitor. The shale sample was crushed and sieved by 6–10 mesh sizes. About 50 g of the crushed shale was mixed with 0.35 l of 2 wt% (100 g of fluid formulation) solution of inhibitor in 0.5 l vessel. The vessel was hot-rolled in hot-roller oven at 150 °C for 16 h, and the mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature, filtered, washed with DI water, and dried in a vacuum oven at 105 °C. The dried shale was sieved via 40-mesh sieve and the weight of the sieve-retained shale was recorded as M1. The percentage recovery was calculated as follows [57].
(2)
R e c o v e r y =
M 1
50
100
%

The drilling fluid used for the rheological study was prepared using the formula presented in Table 1 . After 24 h aging of the fluid at room temperature, the plastic viscosity, apparent viscosity, and yield point of the fluid were determined using Fann 50SL rotational viscometer in line with the 13B standard procedure of American Petroleum Institute (API) [58]. The plastic viscosity (PV), apparent viscosity (AV), and the yield point (YP) were calculated using the following formulas.
(3)
P V =
θ
600
-
θ
300
(4)
A V =
θ
600
2
(5)
Y P =
θ
300
-
θ
600
2

The composite activated carbon-dendrimer/polyvinylpyrrolidone (AC-D/PVP) was achieved via free radical polymerization. The FTIR spectrum of the composites and the AC-D are shown in Fig. 3 . The absorption peaks suggest the character of the bond present in the structure of the two entities. Peaks in both AC-D and AC-D/PVP at around 3426 cm−1 are associated with N-H stretching [55]. The broad nature of AC-D/PVP spectra around this region may be due to residual water molecule OH vibration stretching mode. For AC-D/PVP, the peak at 1633 cm−1 is due to the CO stretch of the pyrrolidone group [59]. The peak at around 1465 is a result of CH2 bending of the polymer chain backbone while the one at 1289 cm−1 may be ascribed to CN stretching vibration. Pertaining to ACD, the peak at 2920 cm−1 is a characteristic feature of CH stretching. The CO stretching appeared at 1720 cm−1 and the peaks at 1008 cm−1 and 1196 cm−1 are properties of CN stretching vibration of the dendrimer [60]. The FTIR spectra reveal the successful production of the intended composite.

Fig. 4 shows the 1HNMR spectrum of the composites. This study was undertaken to further complement the result of the IR spectrum generated. The single peak at 4.65 ppm indicates the presence of water which might be from the solvent or residual water molecule in the material. A chemical shift of 3.5 ppm is the peak of H on the polymer backbone carbon atom close to the pyrrolidine nitrogen. The proton on the polymer backbone second carbon atom which is quite shielded shows at 1.5–1.6 ppm. This peak may also be ascribed to the second carbon to the N atom in the pyrrolidine group [60]. The peak around 3.2–3.4 ppm belongs to the proton on the pyrrolidine carbon next to the nitrogen atom. The peak within 1.8–2.0 ppm is for the proton on the pyrrolidine carbon next to the CO group [60]. The protons on the two C atoms (N-C-C-N) of the ethylenediamine group in the dendrimer overlap with that of the proton on the carbon next to the nitrogen atom of pyrrolidine at 3.2 ppm [61]. The protons on the two carbons of the methacrylate group of the dendrimer appear at 2.6 ppm [62]. The result of the 1HNMR further complements the success of the composite formation as observed in the FTIR spectra.

The ability of shale inhibitors not suffering significant degradation during drilling operation is key to their design [63]. Typically, low-temperature drilling is usually in the range of 50 °C–120 °C while temperature beyond 120 °C is observed for high-temperature drillings [66]. Based on this, the prepared AC-D/PVP was examined for its thermal stability. As presented in Fig. 5 , the material reflects great thermal property. It undergoes a gradual loss of weight as the temperature increases. The steep falling from the room temperature to around 150 °C which further continues with a slight reduction in weight accounts for 15.23 % loss is due to the liberation of the residual moisture in the material [64]. The slight diminishing nature of the curve at around 200 °C could be attributed to further shrinking of the composite due to additional loss of intercalated vapor. The major degradation of the pyrrolidone component of the materials occurs at around 450 °C with a sharp fall of the thermal analysis graph involving depolymerization and breakdown of the polyvinylpyrrolidone and the dendrimer content of the composite. This led to the loss of about 81.56 % quantity of the substance under study up to 525 °C upon which the weight of material remains constant associating with the carbon residue of the AC.

The anti-swelling capacity of 2 wt% AC-D/PVP was evaluated and its activity was compared to 10 wt% of potassium chloride (KCl). It should be noted that KCl is the conventional inhibitor with distinguished performance commonly use in the drilling industry for oil and gas well [22]. Fig. 6 a shows the percentage anti-swelling strength of solution AC-D/PVP and KCl on sodium bentonite (Na-Bt). The response of Na-Bt in the solutions was compared to its behavior in water. The value recorded for pure water is around 33.7 %. Shales are known to suffer swelling due to their affinity for water in water-based drilling fluids based on the montmorillonite composition. Montmorillonite is the major content of Na-Bt accounting for about 80 % of its weight. This makes Na-Bt undergoes rapid swelling and dispersion when it got in contact with water. The 10 wt% KCl solution gave a 55.4 % anti-swelling ratio while the 2 wt% solution of the AC-D/PVP with approximately 90.1 % displayed a greater inhibiting characteristic. Therefore, the incorporation of 2 wt% of AC-D/PVP and even lesser concentration when incorporated in water-based drilling mud could effectively address the problem of shale hydration and borehole instability. This is because shale has far less montmorillonite content than Na-Bt. This tested concentration of the composite could be used efficiently for drilling a highly sensitive shale.

Shale dispersion test is critical in assessing the proficiency of inhibitors to hinder shale swelling and dissipation when in contact with the water component of the water-based fluid [19]. The result of the shale recovery test in this study is shown in Fig. 6b. Shale cuttings suffered significant fluffing in ordinary water compared to the solutions of 10 wt% KCl and the 2 wt% AC-D/PVP. About 35.1 % of the cuttings were recovered at the end of the evaluation in water due to the sensitivity of shale to water. The highest recovery was recorded in the solution of the 2 wt% AC-D/PVP amounting to 92.2 % which is a great achievement comparing to previous studies of some other materials graphene, polymeric and nonpolymeric shale inhibiting materials [34,9,8]. The 10 wt% KCl solution exhibited an intermediate recovery value of around 63 %. The notable high shale recovery of cuttings in the 2 wt% solution of AC-D/PVP indicates that it does not only minimize water invasion into shale formation but ultimately preserves its integrity from dispersion in the fluid formulation.

The physical observation showing the response of Na-Bt to inhibitor solution as well as water and KCl was undertaken via the immersion study. It is one of the important techniques designed to explore potential inhibitor in drilling activity [7]. The image of the Na-Bt pellet appearance immediately was immersed in KCl and AC-D/PVP solution as well as after 24 h are presented in Fig. 7 a–f. The pellet underwent substantial swelling and dispersion within 24 h (Fig. 7d) which demonstrates the rapid reaction of water with the bentonite montmorillonite. The bentonite pellet did not experience swelling but dispersed into the solution due to the replacement of the sodium ions (Na+) in the Na-Bt with the potassium ion (K+) in the solution which helps in bring the clay silica sheets together (Fig. 7b and e). As shown in Fig. 7c and f, the pellet shape is still preserved after 24 h in the solution of 2 wt% AC-D/PVP. Instead of swelling and/or dispersion as observed in water and KCl solution, the surface contracted leading to the compact of the pellet structure (Fig. 7f).

To explain the mechanism of action of AC-D/PVP, the SEM imaging of the Na-Bt exposed to the material solution was carried out. The SEM images and EDX spectra of the Na-Bt pellet and the pellet in contact with the AC-D/PVP solution are depicted in Fig. 8 . EDX elemental analysis was used to confirm the types of elements in the shale before and after treatment within the inhibitor for 1 day. The untreated pellet’s surface seems rough with openings owing to the clay silica discs (Fig. 8a). It is also evident with the high peaks of silicon and other clay elemental compositions as seen in the EDX spectrum (Fig. 8b). After interaction with AC-D/PVP, there is a formation of a film of polymer on Na-Bt surface, as shown in Fig. 8c. This results to the reduction of peaks for the majority of the clay composition, and an increase in the peak of carbon from the material (Fig. 8d). The nano-size and the surface energy of activated carbon (AC) particles of the composite make it capable to plug nano-interlayer spacing in the clay, and it could assist in further adherence of the PVP polymer firmly on the clay surface [3]. The dendrimer group on the AC may be responsible for the contraction of the clay structure due to its ability of electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding formation with clay particles which tends to reduce its Zeta potential [46]. The dendrimer could also form hydrogen bonds with the oxygen and nitrogen atoms on the pyrrolidine group of the polymer which could lead to the strengthening of polymer film formation, adsorption, and adherence to the clay surface [1]. The slight hydrophobic nature of the pyrrolidone rings of the polymer and the hydrodynamic volume of the polymer itself and the dendrimer could result in the large volume of hydrogen bond which additionally enhances the inhibition of clay swelling. The result of the SEM image, as well as the above explanation based on literature, suggest the possible mechanism of the AC-D/PVP composite when utilizing in WBM for drilling as illustrated in Fig. 9 . The material has the viability to find applicable in the real usage as a shale inhibitor in water-based drilling fluid due to its vigor to form an intense hydrogen bonding, formation of the protective film, and physical plugging of the clay nanopore.

The rheology measurement of the three formulated fluids is shown in Table 1. The outcome of the fluid’s rheology assessment is presented in Table 2 . The introduction of AC-D and AC-D/PVP does not significantly affect the rheological properties of the drilling fluids which indicate that AC-D/PVP could be used to replace KCl solution as a shale inhibitor in water-based drilling fluids.

The shale swelling stabilization and inhibition of an environmentally pleasing activated carbon-based composite were studied. The AC-D/PVP was synthesized and characterized by various spectroscopic and thermal techniques. The material showed a remarkable effort toward effective alleviation of shale instability during a drilling operation in a water-based mud. It has a well anti-swelling and a high shale dispersion property of 90.1 % and 92.2 % respectively. These values are higher than 55.4 % and 63 % respectively for KCl solutions which is the conventional inhibitor in the oil and gas industry. The immersion and SEM imaging study unveiled the mechanism of the inhibitive trait of the material ascribing to the synergistic plugging of clay pores as well as the formation of polymer film and hydrogen bond between the formulation and the clay mineral. Besides, the high hydrodynamic volume of dendrimer and the polymer resulting in a large volume of hydrogen bonding further contribute to its tenacity to inhibit the hydration of clay with good sustainability. Polyvinylpyrrolidone moiety of the prepared matrix has a property of moderate viscosity, which is crucial to the application of polymer as a component of cleaner drilling fluid by not significantly affect the rheological parameters of the mud.
Mukaila A. Ibrahim1 performed the experiments and results with draft writing. Tawfik A. Saleh reviewed the paper, supervision and checked the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improved shale hydration inhibition with combination of gelatin and KCl or EPTAC, an environmentally friendly inhibitor for water-based drilling fluids
J. Appl. Polym. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Jiang, X. Li, H. Zhu, L. Yang, Y. Li, T. Wang, and X. Wu, "Improved shale hydration inhibition with combination of gelatin and KCl or EPTAC, an environmentally friendly inhibitor for water-based drilling fluids," J. Appl. Polym. Sci., 136 (22) 2019 47585.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evolution of pore structure in gas shale related to structural deformation
Fuel

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Liang, Z. Wang, L. Gao, C. Li, and H. Li, "Evolution of pore structure in gas shale related to structural deformation," Fuel, 197 (2017) 310-319.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and characterization of lignosulfonate/acrylamide graft copolymers and their application in environmentally friendly water-based drilling fluid
J. Petroleum Sci. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Abdollahi, M. Pourmahdi, and A. R. Nasiri, "Synthesis and characterization of lignosulfonate/acrylamide graft copolymers and their application in environmentally friendly water-based drilling fluid," Journal of Petroleum Science and Engineering, 171 (2018) 484-494.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phase behavior and flow in shale nanopores from molecular simulations
Fluid Phase Equilib.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Jin and A. Firoozabadi, "Phase behavior and flow in shale nanopores from molecular simulations," Fluid Phase Equilib., 430 (2016) 156-168.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances in inhibitive water-based drilling fluids - can they replace oil-based muds?
Proceedings - SPE International Symposium on Oilfield Chemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Patel, E. Stamatakis, S. Young, and J. Friedheim, "Advances in inhibitive water-based drilling fluids - Can they replace oil-based muds?," in Proceedings - SPE International Symposium on Oilfield Chemistry, (2007) 614-621.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of non-damaging and inhibitive water based oil well drilling fluids
Pet. Sci. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Mahto, P. Srikanth, and B. V. Krishna, "Development of non-damaging and inhibitive water based oil well drilling fluids," Pet. Sci. Technol., 31 (7)(2013) 721-726.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation and performance properties of polymer latex SDNL in water-based drilling fluids for drilling troublesome shale formations
J. Nat. Gas Sci. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Xu, Z. Qiu, W. Huang, and X. Zhao, "Preparation and performance properties of polymer latex SDNL in water-based drilling fluids for drilling troublesome shale formations," Journal of Natural Gas Science and Engineering, 37 (2017) 462-470.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Drilling mud rheology and the API recommended measurements
Proceedings - SPE Production Operations Symposium

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. E. Clark, "Drilling mud rheology and the API recommended measurements," in Proceedings - SPE Production Operations Symposium, (1995) 933-941.
